front: Poem: Satan declares 'Better to reign in Hell than serve in Heav'n' to Beelzebub
Poem: Hell's flames give off 'no light but rather darkness visible'
Poem: Book III opens praising 'holy light, offspring of Heav'n first-born' and describes the author's own blindness
Poem: Satan 'felt how awful goodness is' after Zephon does not recognize him
Poem: Eve 'formed for softness and sweet attractive grace' follows the advice of a serpent 'with burnished neck of verdant gold'
Author: Stanley Fish's Surprised by Sin argues this poet's magnum opus provokes 'fallen responses'; C.S. Lewis wrote a book-length preface to the same work
Author: William Blake claimed this poet was 'of the Devil's party without knowing it'
Author: T.S. Eliot complained this poet wrote English 'like a dead language'; Ezra Pound said this poet was 'chock a block with Latin'
Author: Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar wrote that 'all women writers have been to some extent' this poet's 'daughters' in The Madwoman in the Attic
Poem: the speaker laments 'that one Talent which is death to hide' and asks 'Doth God exact day-labour, light denied?'
back: "When I Consider How My Light Is Spent" (John Milton)
work: Sonnets (including 'When I Consider How My Light Is Spent')

front: Poem: two sonnets addressed to Cyriack Skinner; one describes 'liberty's defense' as 'my noble task, of which all Europe talks'
work: Sonnets (including 'When I Consider How My Light Is Spent')

front: Poem: a sonnet (likely about Katherine Woodcock) ends 'I wak'd, she fled, and day brought back my night'
back: "Methought I Saw My Late Espoused Saint" (John Milton)
work: Sonnets (including 'When I Consider How My Light Is Spent')

front: Poem: 'Avenge, O Lord, thy slaughtered saints, whose bones / Lie scattered on the Alpine mountains cold'
back: "On the Late Massacre in Piedmont" (John Milton)
work: Sonnets (including 'When I Consider How My Light Is Spent')

front: Poem: ends 'Tomorrow to fresh woods, and pastures new'; inspired Thomas Wolfe's Look Homeward, Angel title; mourns drowning of Edward King
Poem: describes 'scrannel pipes of wretched straw'; a 'two-handed engine' will 'smite once, and smite no more'
Play: the giant Harapha challenges the blind, chained title character; Manoa tries to negotiate ransom
Play: the title character pulls down a temple of Dagon; the chorus departs with 'calm of mind all passion spent'
Play: the title character laments 'My self, my Sepulcher, a moving Grave' and his 'race of glory run, and race of shame'
Play: the water nymph Sabrina frees a Lady trapped in a chair at Ludlow Castle
work: Comus (A Masque Presented at Ludlow Castle)

front: Play: two brothers receive the magical plant haemony from a spirit disguised as the shepherd Thyrsis
work: Comus (A Masque Presented at Ludlow Castle)

front: Play: the title character, son of Bacchus and Circe, possesses a liquor that turns humans' heads into beasts
work: Comus (A Masque Presented at Ludlow Castle)

front: Work: declares 'he who destroys a good book, kills reason itself' in a tract against pre-publication censorship
Work: title comes from an Isocrates oration named for a hill in Athens (the Areopagus)
Work: imagines the 'sad friends' of 'the virgin Truth' seeking the 'thousand pieces' of her body like Isis gathering the limbs of Osiris
Author: William Blake wrote a two-book epic titled for this poet in which his spirit enters Blake's body through his foot; it opens 'And did those feet in ancient time'
Author: defended regicide in Eikonoklastes (response to Eikon Basilike) and The Tenure of Kings and Magistrates
Author: wrote Divorce Tracts (Tetrachordon, Doctrine and Discipline of Divorce) after wife Mary Powell left him
Author: Tony Harrison's 'On Not Being' this poet analogizes speaking with 'glottals' and 'Ludding morphemes' to smashing looms
Poem: calls Shakespeare a 'son of Memory, great heir of fame' under a 'star-ypointing pyramid'
Author: wrote a Greek verse calling William Marshall's portrait 'the wretched picture of a wretched artist'; Marshall unknowingly engraved the insult
